tweets.title: R to @sidhant: NIA Press Release The National Investigation Agency (NIA) on Monday filed charges against Pakistan-based terrorist and LeT/TRF chief & founder Hafiz Saeed in the dastardly Pahalgam terror attack case.   In its supplementary chargesheet filed before the NIA Special Court, Jammu, the anti-terror agency has charged Hafiz Saeed in his individual capacity and also as the chief of the banned Lashkar-e-Taiba (LeT) terror outfit and its active proxy organisation The Resistance Front (TRF).   The accused has been charged under various sections of Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023 and the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act, 1967. NIA has also invoked the penal section against the accused for waging war against India and hatching a conspiracy from across the border in the chargesheet.   The chargesheet, filed in continuation of the original 1,597-page chargesheet, provides details of Pakistan’s conspiracy, Hafeez Saeed’s role, and supporting evidence collected by NIA in the case through meticulous scientific investigation and on-ground examination.   In its earlier chargesheet filed on 15th December 2025, NIA had chargesheeted Pakistani handler terrorist Sajid Jatt as an accused, along with three terrorists killed by security forces during Operation Mahadev in July 2025, as well as two arrested accused. It had also charged the proscribed LeT/TRF terrorist organisation as a legal entity for its role in planning, facilitating, and executing the Pahalgam terror attack.   The deadly attack in Pahalgam in Jammu & Kashmir on 22nd April, 2025 involved religion-based targeted killings by the Pak-sponsored terrorists. Twenty-five innocent tourists and one local civilian were killed in the attack. An FIR (no. 25/2025) was initially registered by Police Station Pahalgam, district Anantnag. After initial investigation by the Jammu and Kashmir Police, the case was transferred to NIA by the Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India.   NIA continues to probe the case RC-02/2025/NIA/JMU to unravel the complete conspiracy by Pakistan, which has been actively sponsoring terrorism on Indian soil from across the border.
tweets.content:
NIA Press Release

The National Investigation Agency (NIA) on Monday filed charges against Pakistan-based terrorist and LeT/TRF chief & founder Hafiz Saeed in the dastardly Pahalgam terror attack case.
 
In its supplementary chargesheet filed before the NIA Special Court, Jammu, the anti-terror agency has charged Hafiz Saeed in his individual capacity and also as the chief of the banned Lashkar-e-Taiba (LeT) terror outfit and its active proxy organisation The Resistance Front (TRF).
 
The accused has been charged under various sections of Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023 and the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act, 1967. NIA has also invoked the penal section against the accused for waging war against India and hatching a conspiracy from across the border in the chargesheet.
 
The chargesheet, filed in continuation of the original 1,597-page chargesheet, provides details of Pakistan’s conspiracy, Hafeez Saeed’s role, and supporting evidence collected by NIA in the case through meticulous scientific investigation and on-ground examination.
 
In its earlier chargesheet filed on 15th December 2025, NIA had chargesheeted Pakistani handler terrorist Sajid Jatt as an accused, along with three terrorists killed by security forces during Operation Mahadev in July 2025, as well as two arrested accused. It had also charged the proscribed LeT/TRF terrorist organisation as a legal entity for its role in planning, facilitating, and executing the Pahalgam terror attack.
 
The deadly attack in Pahalgam in Jammu & Kashmir on 22nd April, 2025 involved religion-based targeted killings by the Pak-sponsored terrorists. Twenty-five innocent tourists and one local civilian were killed in the attack. An FIR (no. 25/2025) was initially registered by Police Station Pahalgam, district Anantnag. After initial investigation by the Jammu and Kashmir Police, the case was transferred to NIA by the Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India.
 
NIA continues to probe the case RC-02/2025/NIA/JMU to unravel the complete conspiracy by Pakistan, which has been actively sponsoring terrorism on Indian soil from across the border.

tweets.title: Armed Forces Seek Higher Retention For Agniveer The Indian Armed Forces are reportedly seeking to increase the retention of Agniveers after their four-year tenure under the Agnipath scheme. - Indian Navy may seek up to 75% retention -Indian Army and Indian Air Force may seek up to 50% retention The currently approved retention rate remains 25% for all three services. The proposal is driven by the need to retain trained, experienced personnel, particularly those familiar with modern technologies, weapons systems, and operational deployments. No decision has been taken yet. Any change would require approval after consultations with the Department of Military Affairs (DMA).
tweets.content:
Armed Forces Seek Higher Retention For Agniveer

The Indian Armed Forces are reportedly seeking to increase the retention of Agniveers after their four-year tenure under the Agnipath scheme.

- Indian Navy may seek up to 75% retention

-Indian Army and Indian Air Force may seek up to 50% retention

The currently approved retention rate remains 25% for all three services.

The proposal is driven by the need to retain trained, experienced personnel, particularly those familiar with modern technologies, weapons systems, and operational deployments.

No decision has been taken yet. Any change would require approval after consultations with the Department of Military Affairs (DMA).

tweets.title: #Exclusive : 🇮🇳 IHQ MoD(Navy) has initiated a process to develop Containerised Theatre Level Sonar (CTLS) and the doctrine behind it is the real story. This isn’t a hull-mounted sonar for a new ASW frigate. It’s a containerised VDS + twin thin-line array package, deployable on ANY suitable IN ship/auxiliary the only mandatory ship-fit is a certified A-frame + winch. Everything else plugs in. Combined with mono/bi/multi-static operating modes, this points to India building toward a networked, ship-agnostic ASW sensor grid Think CAPTAS/Nixie-style VDS logic, but boxed for rapid deployment on OPVs and auxiliaries rather than dedicated ASW combatants. Why it matters: with frigate/corvette production timelines stretched, this lets IN turn any hull with deck space into an ad-hoc ASW node A force-multiplier that decouples ASW coverage from new hull construction.

tweets.title: The Cost of War: Russia's Wartime Economy The Russian Finance Ministry confirmed that military costs now consume 46% of all government funds, fundamentally altering the nation's financial stability. The Budget Squeeze: Nearly half of all state spending goes to the military, forcing heavy funding cuts for healthcare, education, and infrastructure. Fuel Shortages: Continuous drone strikes on major oil refineries have crippled fuel production, leading to domestic rationing and export restrictions. Economic Risk: Analysts warn the economy is now unsustainably dependent on defense spending, creating a structural trap that threatens long-term stability.

tweets.title: DIRCM is an important capability if an aircraft is expected to operate in environments where shoulder-fired MANPADS are a credible threat. It can also improve survivability against short-range IR-guided air-to-air missiles, although its effectiveness against modern imaging infrared (IIR) seekers is still not well understood in the public domain / attributable data. One design trade-off rarely discussed is RCS. Those large spherical DIRCM turrets are external protrusions, so it is worth asking whether they introduce a measurable radar cross-section penalty. The real question isn’t whether there is any increase, but whether the survivability gained against IR threats far outweighs any marginal increase in RCS. That’s an interesting engineering trade-off.
tweets.content:
DIRCM is an important capability if an aircraft is expected to operate in environments where shoulder-fired MANPADS are a credible threat. It can also improve survivability against short-range IR-guided air-to-air missiles, although its effectiveness against modern imaging infrared (IIR) seekers is still not well understood in the public domain / attributable data.

One design trade-off rarely discussed is RCS. Those large spherical DIRCM turrets are external protrusions, so it is worth asking whether they introduce a measurable radar cross-section penalty.

The real question isn’t whether there is any increase, but whether the survivability gained against IR threats far outweighs any marginal increase in RCS. That’s an interesting engineering trade-off.

tweets.title: I understand every argument in favour of Rafale, and I agree that India needs Rafale today, if not yesterday. But here’s the question I’m trying to answer. If the Government signs a deal for 94 Rafales to be manufactured in India, and deliveries begin in 2032, then even at a production rate of around 12 aircraft per year, the fleet would grow roughly like this: 2032: Deliveries begin 2033: 13 aircraft inducted 2034: 25 2035: 37 2036: 49 2037: 61 2038: 73 2039: 85 2040: 94 How does this solve the IAF’s immediate squadron shortage? The real question isn’t whether Rafale is an excellent aircraft.. it is. The question is: what is the bridge between now and 2032–2040? and this is F4 (not F5) P.S. I’ve deliberately simplified the induction timeline by assuming a near-steady production rate. In reality, there would likely be a production ramp-up in the initial years and a ramp-down towards the end, so the exact yearly numbers would differ. The point is to illustrate the overall timeline, not predict the precise delivery schedule.
tweets.content:
I understand every argument in favour of Rafale, and I agree that India needs Rafale today, if not yesterday.

But here’s the question I’m trying to answer.

If the Government signs a deal for 94 Rafales to be manufactured in India, and deliveries begin in 2032, then even at a production rate of around 12 aircraft per year, the fleet would grow roughly like this:

2032: Deliveries begin
2033: 13 aircraft inducted
2034: 25
2035: 37
2036: 49
2037: 61
2038: 73
2039: 85
2040: 94

How does this solve the IAF’s immediate squadron shortage?

The real question isn’t whether Rafale is an excellent aircraft.. it is. The question is: what is the bridge between now and 2032–2040? and this is F4 (not F5)

P.S. I’ve deliberately simplified the induction timeline by assuming a near-steady production rate. In reality, there would likely be a production ramp-up in the initial years and a ramp-down towards the end, so the exact yearly numbers would differ. The point is to illustrate the overall timeline, not predict the precise delivery schedule.

tweets.title: My two cents on rocket force debate : Most commentators from the IAF are naturally looking at this through an Air Force lens, and that’s fair. Their concerns about duplication, joint planning, and command structures are valid. But I think one important question is often missed. If India’s ambition is to eventually field thousands of conventional ballistic, cruise, and hypersonic missiles, can missile warfare continue to be just another responsibility within the IAF, competing with fighters, AWACS, tankers, transport aircraft, and air defence for budgets and attention? At that scale, the debate changes. It isnt about IAF vs Rocket Force instead its question of whether missile warfare has become important enough to deserve its own doctrine, procurement priorities, and long-term institutional focus.
tweets.content:
My two cents on rocket force debate :

Most commentators from the IAF are naturally looking at this through an Air Force lens, and that’s fair. Their concerns about duplication, joint planning, and command structures are valid.

If India’s ambition is to eventually field thousands of conventional ballistic, cruise, and hypersonic missiles, can missile warfare continue to be just another responsibility within the IAF, competing with fighters, AWACS, tankers, transport aircraft, and air defence for budgets and attention?

At that scale, the debate changes.

It isnt about IAF vs Rocket Force instead its question of whether missile warfare has become important enough to deserve its own doctrine, procurement priorities, and long-term institutional focus.

tweets.title: Yes, no country can eliminate imports overnight. But every import should hurt our self-respect. We should feel ashamed that we still depend on others for critical technologies, and let that discomfort drive us to build them ourselves. The goal should be simple: zero critical imports and as PM says an Atmanirbhar India. Self-respect creates ambition. Ambition creates capability. That sense of self-respect must trickle from the new generation of leaders, who refuse to accept dependence as normal, to the older generation that grew up believing it was inevitable.
tweets.content:
Yes, no country can eliminate imports overnight. But every import should hurt our self-respect. We should feel ashamed that we still depend on others for critical technologies, and let that discomfort drive us to build them ourselves.

The goal should be simple: zero critical imports and as PM says an Atmanirbhar India.

Self-respect creates ambition. Ambition creates capability. That sense of self-respect must trickle from the new generation of leaders, who refuse to accept dependence as normal, to the older generation that grew up believing it was inevitable.

Defence Matrix (@Defencematrix1)

For country like India, Imports can't stop overnight since we were world's largest arms Importer for many decades, but a transition should come, where a roadmap is needed on where and how Imports should be done, Clarity on Imports should be there and companies which are actually trying to develop something locally on their own should also get rewarded. Screwdrivergiri also can't stop overnight but there should also be a clarity on the real nature of manufacturing. White labelling should be a crime till the system is completely localised, even in that case, till it is evolved with R&D for local requirement. Domestic name should not be given till it is domesticated and the issue is not just with private sector, it's equally with PSUs, in-fact PSUs survived on the very same thing, so taking this from private sector would be wrong, but the method should be corrected, hope this online discussion and debates should help in this cause.

tweets.title: (If true) Based on this experience skynex will improve and become a better system. The cycle goes like Deploy -> learn -> refine -> redeploy
tweets.content:
(If true) Based on this experience skynex will improve and become a better system.

The cycle goes like

Deploy -> learn -> refine -> redeploy

Chay Bowes (@BowesChay)

Germanys "Skynex batteries" were deployed to defend an industrial area in Ukraine, but failed dismally.

They included eight 35mm anti-aircraft installations, two radar stations, and two command posts. However, it all went very wrong very fast.

In the first minutes of the battle, three of the eight installations failed: the hydraulics malfunctioned, the tracking radar broke down, and the loading mechanism jammed. As a result, according to the Ukrainian side, only two installations were able to even track aerial targets.

The report's conclusions indicate that the Rheinmetall system demonstrated "low readiness for combat use" and its actual characteristics do not match those "claimed by the manufacturer"

Rheinmetall said any possible failures were attributed to "errors by Ukrainian crews"
